source: Curated from FSF compatibility matrix, SPDX license expressions spec, and published legal analysis
methodology: Each entry verified against license text and FSF/OSI published guidance. Confidence reflects legal certainty.

entries.conditions: Both are permissive. Include both copyright notices and the Apache-2.0 NOTICE file. Combined work may be distributed under either license's terms for respective components.
Both are permissive with functionally identical terms. Include both copyright notices.
Both are permissive with nearly identical terms. Include both copyright notices.
MIT code can be incorporated into GPL-2.0+ projects. The combined work must be distributed under GPL-2.0-or-later. MIT's terms (notice preservation) are compatible with GPL requirements.
MIT code can be incorporated into GPL-3.0+ projects. The combined work must be distributed under GPL-3.0-or-later. MIT imposes no additional restrictions beyond what GPL permits.
MIT code can be incorporated into GPL-3.0-only projects. The combined work must be distributed under GPL-3.0-only. MIT imposes no additional restrictions beyond what GPL permits.
MIT code can be incorporated into GPL-2.0-only projects. The combined work must be distributed under GPL-2.0-only. MIT imposes no additional restrictions beyond what GPL permits.

Apache-2.0 code can be included in GPL-3.0-only projects. GPL-3.0 Section 7 explicitly permits additional patent termination clauses, resolving the conflict that exists with GPL-2.0. Combined work distributed under GPL-3.0-only.
BSD-2-Clause code can be incorporated into GPL-3.0-only projects. BSD imposes no additional restrictions beyond what GPL permits. Combined work under GPL-3.0-only.
BSD-3-Clause code can be incorporated into GPL-3.0-only projects. The non-endorsement clause does not impose additional restrictions under GPL-3.0 Section 7. Combined work under GPL-3.0-only.

Incompatible. Apache-2.0's patent termination clause (Section 3) constitutes an additional restriction under GPL-2.0 Section 6, which prohibits imposing further restrictions on recipients. The FSF confirms this incompatibility.

Apache-2.0 code can be included in GPL-3.0+ projects. GPL-3.0 Section 7 explicitly permits additional patent termination clauses, resolving the conflict that exists with GPL-2.0. Combined work distributed under GPL-3.0-or-later.
BSD-2-Clause code can be incorporated into GPL-2.0+ projects. BSD-2-Clause's only requirements (notice retention) are compatible with GPL. Combined work under GPL-2.0-or-later.

MIT code can be included in proprietary software. You must include the MIT copyright notice and permission notice in documentation or source distributions. No requirement to disclose proprietary source code.

Apache-2.0 code can be included in proprietary software. You must include the NOTICE file contents, state changes made to Apache-licensed files, and retain copyright/patent/trademark notices. No requirement to disclose proprietary source code.

Proprietary software may dynamically link to LGPL-2.1 libraries. You must allow users to re-link with modified versions of the LGPL library (dynamic linking satisfies this by default). Distribute the LGPL library source or provide a written offer. Include copyright notices.

Static linking is permitted but you must provide object files or equivalent for your proprietary code sufficient to re-link with a modified version of the LGPL library (Section 6a), or use a shared library mechanism (Section 6b). Distribute LGPL library source. This is more burdensome than dynamic linking.

Proprietary software may dynamically link to LGPL-3.0 libraries. You must allow re-linking with modified library versions and provide Installation Information (per GPL-3.0 Section 6) if distributing in User Products. Distribute library source under LGPL-3.0.

Static linking is permitted. You must provide Minimal Corresponding Source (the LGPL library source, headers, and linking scripts) plus Installation Information for User Products. Convey object code in a form that allows re-linking per LGPL-3.0 Section 4.

MPL-2.0 is file-level copyleft. Proprietary code in separate files can be combined with MPL-2.0 files in a Larger Work. Modified MPL-2.0 files must remain under MPL-2.0 and source must be made available. Your proprietary files are not affected.

EPL-2.0 is module-level copyleft. Proprietary modules can be combined with EPL-2.0 modules in a product. Modified EPL-2.0 modules must have source made available. Your proprietary modules in separate source code modules are not affected.

Incompatible. GPL-2.0-only (without the 'or later' option) requires distribution under GPL version 2 exactly. GPL-3.0-only requires version 3 exactly. Neither license permits distribution under the other's terms. The 'or later' upgrade path is not available.
GPL-2.0-or-later code can be upgraded to GPL-3.0 via the 'or any later version' clause, allowing combination with GPL-3.0-or-later code. Combined work distributed under GPL-3.0-or-later.

Incompatible. AGPL-3.0 requires that all users interacting over a network receive access to the Corresponding Source of the entire combined work. This is fundamentally incompatible with proprietary licensing. Even server-side use without distribution triggers the copyleft obligation.

Incompatible. GPL-3.0 requires the entire combined work to be licensed under GPL-3.0, including complete Corresponding Source. Proprietary code cannot satisfy this requirement while remaining proprietary.

AGPL-3.0 Section 13 explicitly permits combining AGPL code with GPL-3.0 code. The combined work is governed by AGPL-3.0 (the network-use provision extends to the whole). GPL-3.0 code in the combination gains AGPL's network copyleft obligation.
MPL-2.0 Section 3.3 explicitly permits distributing MPL-2.0 code under GPL-2.0+, LGPL-2.1+, or AGPL-3.0. The MPL-2.0 code becomes subject to the GPL's terms in the combined work.

EUPL-1.2 Appendix lists GPL-2.0-or-later as a compatible license. EUPL code can be relicensed under GPL-2.0+ when incorporated into a GPL-licensed Larger Work, per EUPL Article 5 compatibility mechanism.

Incompatible. Apache-2.0 is not listed in the EUPL-1.2 Appendix of compatible licenses. EUPL's copyleft requires derivative works to be distributed under EUPL or a listed compatible license. Apache-2.0 is permissive and does not meet EUPL's copyleft requirements.

CC0-1.0 waives all copyright and related rights to the fullest extent permitted by law, with a fallback permissive license. CC0 code can be incorporated into any project under any license with no conditions. Note: CC0 does not grant patent licenses.

The Unlicense dedicates the work to the public domain and provides a fallback permissive license. Unlicensed code can be incorporated into any project under any license with no conditions. Note: some jurisdictions do not recognize public domain dedications; the fallback license covers those cases.
